title: plexus.docs content and execute_tactus boot prompt
description:
# plexus.docs content and execute_tactus boot prompt

## Purpose

Make the new MCP tool actually usable. Two complementary deliverables:

1. The `plexus.docs.*` content surface — accessible from inside Lua, supports lazy disclosure of API knowledge.
2. The `execute_tactus` tool description / boot prompt — the FIRST and ONLY thing every MCP client receives. Must teach enough for the agent to bootstrap discovery without exceeding its token budget.

## Hard ceiling: 5k tokens for boot prompt + first-turn discovery overhead

Enforced as a regression test (boot prompt size, plus typical first-turn discovery payload, must fit). If we cannot fit a feature in the budget, the feature surfaces via `plexus.docs.get(...)` instead of being inlined. This forces lazy disclosure as a design discipline.

## Boot prompt content (within ceiling)

- Tool name and one-paragraph description of what `execute_tactus` is.
- The single most important rule: `local plexus = require('plexus')` and use `plexus.*` for everything.
- Discovery: `plexus.docs.list()` to find what docs exist; `plexus.docs.get(key)` to fetch one.
- Cost discipline ladder (one paragraph: prefer cheap deterministic tools over LLM agents).
- Budget: how to check `plexus.budget.remaining()`; how to scope sub-budgets with `plexus.budget.with{}`.
- HITL: destructive operations auto-fire `Human.approve`; `no_confirm = true` to bypass.
- Error semantics: `{ ok, value, error }` shape; structured error tables.
- Three small canonical examples (read, predict, evaluate).

## plexus.docs content (lazy-loaded)

- Per-namespace reference (one doc per `plexus.X` namespace, accessible by key).
- Concept docs for harder topics: budget primitive, dispatched ops + handles, sub-agent composition, the cost ladder, the HITL flow, error handling patterns.
- Migration cookbook content (mapping common 64-tool workflows to Lua snippets).
- Most module docs auto-generated from Python type hints + docstrings to keep them in sync with the implementation. Only hand-write what cannot be derived.

## Auto-generation pipeline

The PlexusModule facade methods carry rich Python type hints + structured docstrings. A build step generates:
- `plexus.docs.*` content (per-namespace markdown).
- Lua signatures for IDE / completion (future).
- Boot-prompt API summary (auto-condensed per-namespace one-liner).

This is the cure for "every release becomes a docs-update treadmill." If we hand-maintain six artifacts per feature, we fail. Auto-generate where possible, hand-write only where the auto-generated form is insufficient.

## Estimate

1 week, mostly the auto-generation pipeline and the careful curation of the boot prompt content within the token ceiling.

## Dependencies

- Epic 3 (PlexusModule) determines the surface to be documented.
- Epic 6 (`execute_tactus`) hosts the boot prompt.
- Plexus procedure docs refresh chore (filed under this epic) is a hard prerequisite — current docs reference deprecated `Worker.turn()` patterns that we must not propagate into the new boot prompt.
